summary: This week's strongest agentic AI signal is that the field got more operational. The highest-signal stories were not just new models, but better control planes around models: Codex safety harnesses, usage ceilings for heavy coding-agent users, domain-specific trusted access, and a growing open-source push for provenance, memory, and cheaper backends. Hacker News centered on coding-agent supervision and the real cost of computer use, while GitHub filled up with agent-native filesystems, memory notebooks, evaluation runners, and versioning experiments. The broad pattern is healthy: agents are getting less magical and more infrastructural.

highlights.title: Running Codex safely at OpenAI
highlights.meta: OpenAI RSS · May 8 · sandboxing, approvals, network policies
highlights.why: The strongest shipping signal this cycle is that coding agents are becoming operational products: safety harnesses, approval gates, and telemetry are now part of the core stack rather than an afterthought.

highlights.title: Vibe coding and agentic engineering are getting closer than I'd like
highlights.why: The biggest builder debate of the week is no longer whether agents can write code, but how much review, rollback, and supervision serious teams now need around them.

highlights.title: Higher usage limits for Claude and a compute deal with SpaceX
highlights.meta: Anthropic · May 6 · 507 points / 479 comments on HN
highlights.why: A useful reality check: for heavy coding-agent users, throughput and budget ceilings are starting to matter almost as much as raw model quality.

highlights.title: Advancing voice intelligence with new models in the API
highlights.meta: OpenAI RSS · May 7 · realtime reasoning, translation, transcription
highlights.why: Voice agents keep getting more credible as reasoning, translation, and transcription collapse into one realtime loop instead of three brittle services.

highlights.title: Computer Use is 45x more expensive than structured APIs
highlights.why: One of the clearest practical takeaways this week: raw browser or desktop control should usually be the fallback, while structured tools remain the default for cost and reliability.

highlights.title: AlphaEvolve: Gemini-powered coding agent scaling impact across fields
highlights.meta: Google DeepMind · May 7 · 321 points / 144 comments on HN
highlights.why: A good reminder that the coding-agent recipe is really a search-and-feedback loop that spills into science, optimization, and systems work—not just software engineering.

highlights.title: Scaling Trusted Access for Cyber with GPT-5.5 and GPT-5.5-Cyber
highlights.meta: OpenAI RSS · May 7 · defender-focused trusted access
highlights.why: Specialized access programs for cyber work are a strong sign that frontier models are being packaged as domain-specific agents with gated deployment, not just generic chat.

highlights.title: Launch HN: Nao Labs (YC X25) – Cursor for Data
highlights.why: A fresh signal that coding-agent ideas are rapidly extending into data engineering: schema-aware context, impact diffs, and domain-specific guardrails are replacing generic copilots.

highlights.title: deepclaude
highlights.meta: 1,645 GitHub stars · created May 3 · Claude Code loop on cheaper backends
highlights.why: One of the more interesting open-source reactions to the current moment: keep the agent UX people like, but swap the model backend to radically change economics.

highlights.title: mirage
highlights.meta: 1,480 GitHub stars · created May 6 · unified virtual filesystem for AI agents
highlights.why: A virtual filesystem is exactly the kind of boring-but-important primitive agents need: stable context boundaries, easier provenance, and cleaner tool-mediated state.

highlights.title: Show HN: Git for AI Agents
highlights.why: Builders increasingly want agent-native provenance, checkpoints, and rewind semantics rather than forcing human-oriented Git workflows onto long autonomous runs.

items.title: ableton-live-mcp
items.meta: 160 GitHub stars · created May 3 · MCP bridge for Ableton Live
items.why: A fun but high-signal tangent: MCP is spreading from coding tools into creative desktop software, which is how agent ecosystems become real platforms.

tangentRadar.whyItMatters: The center of gravity is shifting from “can the model do it?” to “how do you supervise, checkpoint, gate, and recover long agent runs?”

tangentRadar.topic: Structured tools beat computer use by default
signals: Computer Use is 45x more expensive than structured APIs
tangentRadar.whyItMatters: Teams still want browser and desktop agents, but the smartest builders are wrapping real software in tool surfaces instead of paying the full reliability tax of GUI control.

tangentRadar.topic: Memory and provenance are becoming first-class
tangentRadar.whyItMatters: Local memory stores, virtual filesystems, and replayable traces are becoming the difference between a clever demo and an agent you can actually trust tomorrow.

tangentRadar.topic: Voice is graduating into a real agent interface
signals: Advancing voice intelligence with new models in the API
How OpenAI delivers low-latency voice AI at scale
tangentRadar.whyItMatters: Once reasoning, transcription, and translation happen inside one low-latency loop, voice stops being a novelty and starts looking like a serious agent shell.

experimentQueue.title: Compare structured APIs vs browser control on one real workflow
experimentQueue.why: The biggest practical debate this week was cost and reliability: when is computer use worth it, and when is it just expensive theater?
experimentQueue.prompt: Take one multi-step workflow you currently automate in the browser and re-run it with a structured API/tool surface. Measure latency, token cost, failure rate, and human recovery effort side by side.

experimentQueue.title: Add an approval + checkpoint layer to a coding agent
experimentQueue.why: Codex safety scaffolding and agent-native versioning both point to the same missing primitive: stop, inspect, approve, and rewind.
experimentQueue.prompt: Wrap a coding agent loop with explicit checkpoints after planning, file edits, and command execution. Store diffs plus tool traces so you can replay or roll back any step.

experimentQueue.title: Stress-test a local memory stack
experimentQueue.why: Memory repos and virtual filesystems are proliferating, but most people still have not tested which retrieval/state layout actually helps over long tasks.
experimentQueue.prompt: Run the same 30- to 60-minute task with three memory setups: plain conversation buffer, vector retrieval, and filesystem-backed episodic notes. Compare recovery after interruption and factual consistency.

experimentQueue.title: Prototype a voice-first agent for one repetitive support flow
experimentQueue.why: Realtime voice infrastructure is getting strong enough that the interface—not the model—may be the next differentiator.
experimentQueue.prompt: Build a narrow voice agent with one approval gate and one backend tool. Measure barge-in handling, end-to-end latency, and whether users finish the task faster than with chat.

method: Refreshed the baseline generator, then replaced it with a hand-curated payload using verified signals from OpenAI RSS, Anthropic announcements, Hacker News/Algolia metrics, and GitHub repository metadata.
Prioritized real engagement, recency, and direct agent relevance over noisy low-point stories and unreliable Reddit scraping.
Kept the page concise by favoring stories that changed how people build, supervise, evaluate, or ship agents this week.
